title: Quick Start
content: In this guide you will write your first Q-OS WASM module in Rust, compile it to wasm32-unknown-unknown, deploy it to a running local edge node, and watch a guest check in from a mobile browser — all in under 5 minutes.

title: System Overview
content: Q-OS is a layered, local-first compute platform. Every component is designed to operate independently of the internet. The four core layers below interact over in-process function calls and LAN sockets — never cloud APIs.

title: Edge Node (Rust Daemon)
content: The Q-OS Edge Node (`qos-runtime`) is a single, statically compiled Rust binary. It orchestrates the entire lifecycle of a physical interaction: terminating HTTP/WebSocket connections, booting WASM sandboxes, managing state, and finding peers.

title: Sled State Store
content: Q-OS uses sled as its primary embedded database. Sled is a high-performance, embedded, lock-free Bw-Tree. It acts as the single source of truth for edge node state.

title: WebSocket Telemetry
content: Q-OS is designed to operate offline, but when a network is available, it provides a high-performance WebSocket telemetry stream at /api/v1/stream. This powers the Admin HUD with zero-latency updates.

title: CRDT Sync Protocol
content: In larger venues, you might deploy multiple Q-OS edge nodes (e.g., one at the entrance, one at the bar). These nodes must synchronize state without relying on a cloud backend.

title: Overview
content: The Q-OS host ABI exposes a minimal set of extern "C" functions that your WASM module can import. There is no separate library to link — the host runtime injects these symbols at instantiation time.

title: qos_abi::read_input
content: Reads the JSON payload provided by the host environment (typically passed from the guest's Micro-UI upon scanning the QR code).

title: qos_abi::write_output
content: Sends the final receipt payload from the WASM module back to the host, which is then routed to the guest's Micro-UI and the Admin HUD via an EXECUTION_RECEIPT WebSocket event.

title: qos_abi::state_get
content: Retrieves the value for a given key from the Edge Node's Sled database.

title: qos_abi::state_set
content: Writes a byte array to a specific key in the Sled state store. This is the primary way a WASM module causes a persistent side-effect.

title: Error Handling
content: In a #[no_std] WebAssembly environment, error handling must be explicit and deterministic. Q-OS modules must declare a custom panic handler.

title: Overview
content: Each guide walks you through a complete, production-ready WASM module from scratch. Start with the Guestbook to understand the Q-OS ABI, then explore the more advanced guides to build real venue logic.

title: Guest Check-In Module
content: This guide demonstrates how to build a simple but robust Guestbook module. It records the total number of physical visits to a location.

title: Menu & Ordering
content: This guide demonstrates how to process incoming JSON payloads (orders) and append them to a Kitchen Display System (KDS) queue.

title: VIP Validation
content: This module handles single-use ticket validation. It reads a token from the guest, checks if it exists in the valid tokens list, and then burns it so it cannot be re-used.

title: Inventory Sync
content: This guide shows how to decrement inventory counts atomically. In a CRDT mesh network, local subtractions sync across all peer edge nodes automatically.

title: Custom Broadcast Logic
content: Sometimes you want a WASM module to actively push notifications directly to the Admin HUD without storing permanent state. You can do this using the qos_sdk::broadcast module.
